data.title: TD Bank announces expected impact of Charles Schwab's Q1 earnings
data.description:
• TD Bank (NYSE:TD) said on Friday that it expects Charles Schwab's first quarter earnings to translate into approximately CDN $74 million of reported equity in net income of an investment in Schwab for the Bank's fiscal 2025 second quarter.
  • Excluding amortization of acquired intangibles of approximately CDN $9 million after-tax, adjusted equity in net income of an investment in Schwab will be approximately CDN $83 million. 
  • On February 12, 2025, the bank announced the closing of the sale of its entire equity investment in Schwab, consisting of 184.7M shares of Schwab's common stock representing 10.1% economic ownership.

data.title: Vacasa rejects revised proposal from Davidson Kempner
data.description:
• Vacasa (NASDAQ:VCSA) said on Friday that its board has unanimously determined that the revised unsolicited acquisition proposal from Davidson Kempner does not constitute a superior proposal. 
  • The board reaffirmed its recommendation regarding the proposed transaction with Casago. 
  • Vacasa (NASDAQ:VCSA) accepted the offer from Casago at $5.30 per share in March.
  • The Portland-based company had rejected a previous offer from Davidson Kempner as well, as the decision was largely based on Casago’s offer providing greater certainty without requiring amendments to Vacasa's Tax Receivable Agreement.

data.title: CBB Bancorp CEO retires; CFO Richard Koh to replace him
data.description:
• CBB Bancorp (OTCQX:CBBI), the parent company of Commonwealth Business Bank said on Friday that James Hong will retire from his roles as President and Chief Executive Officer of both the company and the bank, effective April 18, 2025.
  • The company also announced that Richard Koh, currently executive Vice President and CFO, will be promoted to President and CEO of both the company and the bank upon Hong’s retirement.

data.title: Netflix may be the antidote for the recession and tariff fears of investors
data.description:
Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) gained after the company topped revenue, EPS, and margin expectations with its Q1 earnings report. The streaming giant noted that there has been no material change to its overall business outlook since the prior earnings report, which is a noteworthy change of pace from many consumer-facing early earnings reporters. 

"We also take some comfort in the fact that entertainment historically has been pretty resilient in tougher economic times," updated Co-CEO Greg Peters added. "Netflix specifically also has been generally quite resilient," he added.

PP Foresight analyst Paolo Pescatore said he believes Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) is in a strong position to handle a recession. "Netflix is an indispensable service in users' lives. It will be the last subscription that users will cancel given the broad and breadth of programming," he advised.

On Wall Street, Bank of America analyst Jessica Reif Ehrlich pointed out that while advertising is a small portion of the Netflix (NFLX) business today, the longer-term prospects for the business are robust due to unique engagement and content. Investments in ad-tech capabilities are expected to drive healthy growth for the advertising business for years to come. 

Ehrlich also called that recent dollar weakness has NFLX revenue tracking toward the higher end of the full-year guidance range. Bank of America reiterated its Buy rating on NFLX and price objective of $1,175. "In our view, Netflix shares will be fueled by continued positive subscriber and earnings momentum in addition to evolving advertising and live opportunities," read the bullish summary. 

Elsewhere, Evercore ISI analyst Mark Mahaney had his eyes on the Netflix (NFLX) margin guidance. He said it was hard for the firm to see how NFLX could bring down second-half margins so aggressively as to generate the 29% level for the full year, but said it is possible that NFLX is giving itself plenty of "dry powder for an aggressive content spend ramp."

On Seeking Alpha, Investing Group Leader Cestrian Capital Research issued a Hold rating on Netflix (NFLX) after digesting the report, while analyst Brett Ashcroft Green fired off what he called a "spec-buy" in a partial upgrade from the previous Hold rating.

Shares of Netflix (NFLX) rose 3.4% in the postmarket session on Thursday.

data.title: Trump vs Powell: Fed Board chair isn't guaranteed to be FOMC chair
data.description:
President Donald Trump has ramped up the pressure on Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell for not cutting interest rates. But a Trump replacement wouldn't have a magic wand to lower rates or even lead monetary policy.

The Fed Board of Governors and the Federal Open Market Committee (which sets rates) are separate and don't have to have the same leader. 

There is no legal obligation for the FOMC to elect the chair of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve (which is what the colloquial title of Fed Chair refers to) as the chair of the rate-setting committee. They could simply elect someone else.

The Feud 

Trump lashed out at Powell on social media Thursday, saying he is too late on cutting rates and adding that Powell's "termination cannot come fast enough."

Later that day, the Wall Street Journal reported that Trump has been engaged in coversations for months about the possibility of firing Powell. Former Fed governor Kevin Warsh (who some see as a favorite to eventually replace Powell) has advised against replacing the Fed chief before his term ends, the Journal reported, while Politco says Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has pushed back on the idea, citing the potential impact on global markets with a loss of Fed independence.

"I've been critical of Bessent but I think he walks before they actively fire Powell," economist Dario Perkins said. "He knows the stakes. 'Guarantor' is too strong, but he is on the right side of this." 

But while taking questions on Thursday, Trump asserted that he could get rid of Powell in some way or another.

"Oh he'll leave. If I ask him to, he'll be out of there. I don't think he's doing the job. He's always too late, a little slow," Trump said.

Powell has said he wouldn't leave if Trump asked him to.

New Fed chief doesn't equal lower rates

Much of the current discourse is whether Trump has the authority to fire Powell. The legal precedent has prevented the president from unilaterally firing heads of independent agencies (Supreme Court ruling in Humphrey's Executor v. United States in 1935). But the White House is looking for the Supreme Court to revisit that and clear the way for that authority.

But even if Trump does get a green light and Powell leaves the Fed altogether, the path to lower rates isn't direct. 

In what UBS Chief Economist Paul Donovan has called an "in extremis" case, the FOMC could elect someone besides the new Fed Board chief as its chair.

The FOMC chair is elected at the first meeting of each year. In the case of a departure of an FOMC chair, the election would occur at the following meeting.

In August 1979, then-serving Fed chief G. William Miller was appointed Treasury Secretary by President Jimmy Carter as part of a cabinet shakeup. At the August 14 FOMC meeting Paul Volcker was elected as the new chairman of the rate-setting committee.

The minutes read: "By unanimous vote, the Committee elected Paul A. Volcker to serve as Chairman for the period until the election of a successor at the first meeting of the Committee after February 29, 1980, with the understanding that in the event of the discontinuance of his offical connection with the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System,, he would cease to have any official connection with the Federal Open Market Committee."

Now, Volcker had also been quickly appointed Fed Board chair the week before. But in the case of hypothetical Powell removal, the FOMC members could vote for somebody other than Trump's handpicked replacement like New York Fed President John Williams.

The Journal reported that when the possibilty of Trump replacing Powell was raised six years ago during Trump's first term, Fed leaders decided they would "close ranks" and elect Powell as FOMC chair.

In the case of a standoff, Powell could remain at the head of the FOMC during a legal challenge.

Guaranteeing the votes for a new Trump pick could require a purge of Fed governors (the president does not appoint regional bank presidents). That would be bad optics given the concern about global market reaction.

And ultimately, the FOMC chair is just one vote on rate-setting policy. There is no dovish rebellion in the FOMC that's being put down by Powell. Sure, he has the ability to arm-twist. But it seems unlikely for the whole committee to just set fire to their dot plots and start slashing rates.
